observability_correction: Live aider 0.86.2 recording overrides the earlier doc-based prediction: `/run <cmd>` does NOT write a `> Running <cmd>` blockquote, so runningRE never fires and ZERO Bash tool_results are emitted. aider 0.86.2 instead writes `> Add command output to the chat?` + `> Added N lines of output to the chat.` confirmation blockquotes (ignored by the parser). The ready→working→ready arc still holds (the `#### /run …` line opens the turn; IdleFlush settles it), but the escape is observed ONLY as that state arc, never as a distinct shell-escape event and never as a tool_result. The original '> Running → Bash tool_result fold' rationale below is RETAINED for provenance but is superseded by this correction.
body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
irrlicht_observes: partial

## Reasoning

### Agent supports the primitive (yes)

The scenario's **Primitive exercised** is a shell-escape mechanism — "Claude Code `!cmd`, or analogous." Aider implements the exact same primitive: the in-chat `/run` command runs a shell command locally (no LLM round-trip) and `!` is its documented shorthand alias. Per aider's in-chat command reference, `/run` is "Run a shell command and optionally add the output to the chat (alias: !)". So aider's `!echo hello` is a direct one-to-one analog of Claude Code's `!cmd` shell-escape this scenario is modeled on — not a loose stand-in. (https://aider.chat/docs/usage/commands.html)

### irrlicht observes it — but as a tool call, not as a distinct shell-escape (partial)

The canonical irrlicht primitive is narrower than "can the daemon see anything happen": it is that **the parser handles shell-escape output as something distinct from a formal tool-call/result pair, and still resolves to `ready`**. Against that bar, aider lands at `partial`.

State resolution is correct. The transport is `FilesUnderCWD{Filename: ".aider.chat.history.md", RawLineParser}` (core/adapters/inbound/agents/aider/agent.go:45-51). When the user runs a shell command, aider writes a `> Running <cmd>` blockquote into the markdown history. The parser's `runningRE = ^>\s*Running\s+` matches that line (parser.go:69-70) and routes it through `p.toolCall("Bash")` (parser.go:122-124). A turn was opened by the preceding `#### ` user-prompt line; the synthesized `turn_done` arrives via `IdleFlush` after the 1500ms idle window (parser.go:216-229). Net lifecycle: `ready → working → ready`, with no stuck-in-working state. The user-observable signal the scenario asserts ("state does not stick in `working` after the shell command exits") therefore holds.

What makes this `partial` rather than `yes` is the *shape* of the observation: irrlicht sees the escape only as a state arc, never as a distinct shell-escape event.

### OBSERVABILITY CORRECTION (live aider 0.86.2 recording — supersedes the doc-based prediction below)

The earlier doc-based reading of this cell predicted that aider writes a `> Running <cmd>` blockquote which runningRE (parser.go:69-70) routes through `p.toolCall("Bash")` (parser.go:122-124), folding the escape into a single Bash `tool_result` on the same path as `> Applied edit`. **The live aider 0.86.2 recording proves that prediction WRONG.** For `/run echo hello`, aider 0.86.2 writes NO `> Running` line — it writes a `> Add command output to the chat? (Y)es/(N)o` confirmation prompt followed by `> Added N lines of output to the chat.` Neither matches runningRE, so `p.toolCall("Bash")` NEVER fires and **ZERO Bash tool_results are emitted** for the escape. (`parser_test.go:162-172`'s `TestParser_ToolCall_RunningShell` still pins the runningRE→Bash path for a literal `> Running` line, but aider 0.86.2 simply never produces that line for `/run`.)

What still holds: the `#### /run echo hello` user-prompt line opens a real turn (→working), and ~1500ms later IdleFlush synthesizes turn_done (working→ready). So the **ready→working→ready arc is intact and never sticks** — the load-bearing user-observable signal holds. The escape is observed solely as that arc.

Consequence for the spec: the claudecode-shaped `verify.tool_calls_max: 0` (scenarios.json) **does hold** for aider 0.86.2 in fact (zero tool calls), but for a DIFFERENT reason than claudecode (which deliberately skips its `<bash-input>`/`<bash-stdout>` wrappers): aider's `/run` simply never writes a parseable tool line. The cell stays `partial` because the escape is not surfaced as anything distinct from an ordinary turn — irrlicht cannot tell a `/run` shell-escape apart from a normal prompt arc. The expected.jsonl asserts the arc + final ready and asserts NO tool_result/Bash marker.

### Capability key

The Primitive maps to existing `capabilities.json` keys, not a dedicated `shell_escape` key (none exists in any adapter). The `/run`/`!` surface is gated by `slash_commands: true`; the observation channel would have been `tool_calls: true`, but the live aider 0.86.2 recording shows the `/run` escape emits NO tool call at all (no `> Running` line → runningRE never fires). irrlicht observes the escape only as the surrounding ready→working→ready state arc. Both capability keys are `true` for aider, but `tool_calls` is not the channel through which THIS primitive is observed.

## Verdict implication

`applicable` = yes (agent_supports yes + irrlicht_observes partial). The cell is recipe-eligible; the recorded spec asserts the state arc + final `ready` and asserts NO tool_result/Bash marker (the live aider 0.86.2 recording shows zero tool calls for `/run` — see the observability correction above).
caveats: OBSERVABILITY CORRECTION (live aider 0.86.2): the shell-escape produces NO tool_result. aider 0.86.2's `/run` writes `> Add command output to the chat?` / `> Added N lines of output`, NOT a `> Running <cmd>` line, so runningRE never fires and zero Bash tool_results are emitted. The cell is `partial` because irrlicht cannot distinguish the escape from an ordinary turn arc — it is observed only as ready→working→ready, never as a distinct shell-escape event.

The scenario's claudecode-shaped verify.tool_calls_max: 0 DOES hold for aider 0.86.2 (zero tool calls observed), but for a different reason than claudecode (no parseable tool line written, vs claudecode deliberately skipping its wrappers). The aider recipe/spec asserts only the state topology + final ready, and asserts NO tool_result/Bash marker.

Working→ready latency is idle-driven (IdleFlush after ~1500ms, gated by the detector's 5s working-refresh poll), so the ready transition trails the shell command's exit by up to several seconds rather than firing on the command's completion line.

TEARDOWN-RACE caveat: the final working→ready IdleFlush settle must land before the tmux kill, or the recording ends mid-working and the final ready + process_exited are lost. The recipe's trailing sleep is ≥12s for this reason.

sources.note: In-chat commands: '/run — Run a shell command and optionally add the output to the chat (alias: !)'. Confirms aider's !cmd shell-escape is the direct analog of Claude Code's !cmd.

runningRE ^>\s*Running\s+ (L69-70) → toolCall("Bash") (L122-124) fires ONLY for a literal `> Running` blockquote. CORRECTION (live aider 0.86.2): `/run` writes `> Add command output to the chat?` / `> Added N lines of output` instead — these fall through to the generic blockquote branch (L126-133, ignored), so runningRE never matches and no Bash tool_result is emitted. The `####` user-prompt line (L94-108) opens the turn and IdleFlush turn_done (L216-229) closes it → the ready→working→ready arc is intact with zero tool calls.

TestParser_ToolCall_RunningShell (L162-172): a LITERAL '> Running echo hello' line produces exactly one Bash ToolUse — pins the runningRE→Bash path. But aider 0.86.2 does NOT write that line for `/run` (it writes the `> Add command output…` confirmation pair), so this path is not exercised by a real `/run` escape; the live recording shows zero tool calls.
Source = FilesUnderCWD{.aider.chat.history.md, RawLineParser} (L45-51) — the markdown transcript that carries the '> Running' line the parser keys on.
No shell_escape key; slash_commands:true gates /run, tool_calls:true is the observation channel. Maps the 'shell-escape mechanism' primitive to existing keys.
